speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Elliot Lefkovitz. I'm conducting an interview on March the 6th, 1997, with Stella Faitek in Chicago, Illinois.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, we'll begin with your name and the spelling of your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stella, S-T-E-L-L-A, Gutman Faitek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Gutman is G-U--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- it's-- how is it spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-U-T-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Faitek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Faitek. F-A-I-T-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sura.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sura, a Yiddish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sura, S-U-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sura Gutman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gutman.

speaker: interviewer
text: G-U-T-M-A-N?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other uh, names or spelling? And-- and the spellings of those names. Any other names at all that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later? What did they-- what was your nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bomba. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: B-O-M-B-A?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was very energetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever I did, so they called me Bomba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a bomb?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Twent-- December the 5th, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- you were born in what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw, in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. Let's begin with your family. How many were in your immediate family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two brothers. And I had a younger sister from mine stepmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the-- the two brothers were older or younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Older. I was the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were the-- you were the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- your parents were-- do you remember when they were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I cannot remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- and what kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that he worked for collecting the Jewish organization money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a homemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And uh-- and your home was a-- an orthodox Jewish home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mine father was Orthodox, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And uh, he went regularly to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember going to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to cheder. I went to cheder two years. Then I didn't want to go anymore because the rabbi punished me. He thought that I making noise. And when he punished me I said, I will not go anymore to cheder. I stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what age was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother died--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died when she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 34 years old. I was probably six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she die from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was paralyzed. Lately, she didn't s-- speak at all. She was-- she wasn't walking. I don't remember her walking at all. But I remember when she stopped talking. And then I remember she was dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- in-- the injury or the paralysis, she suffered during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, yeah. No, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. She died before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sorry--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, before-- uh, during World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war w-- yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you know how she was injured? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that mine father used to say that she was washing laundry. Not in a machine. And the window was open. The door was open. It was a draft. From the draft she was paralyzed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your whole childhood, ear-- I mean, the earliest years of your childhood were spent during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about World War I? About those years? Very early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the time, Warsaw was occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything at all about the-- the German-- a German soldier, any contact with a German soldier at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember very well when they occupied Czechoslovakia. And we were talking about that [? she ?] might come to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 10th month, it was, I think they occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first day, not far from me, the school was bombarded. Lots of people were killed then. And we decided, the fourth day of the war, to leave Poland, to leave Warsaw, and to go, walk to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's just-- we'll-- we'll pick up on that. But let's just go back again to World War I. [LAUGHS] And those very early years. As far as the-- the language in your home that was spoken, what-- what was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In mine home, with my daddy, it was Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And between you and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. We talk, always, Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um, then when your-- as far as your-- where-- where you lived in um-- in-- in Warsaw, what was the address? Do you recall the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Mila 29. It's a famous street. you probably read the book, It was not far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the neighborhood? About the other children? Do you remember anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that this was a Jewish neighborhood. Anti-Semitism was very big. We were very often chased on the street. They called, uh, you are a dirty Jew. I remember this. It was very hard. But we managed somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family was poor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- do you remember as a little child, did you ever have any toys of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never had a toy. And when we were even by Korczak, we had there a house of dolls. We were only looking at this. Because so many children, 56 girls, 50 boys, they would break it. So we just looked at the dolls in the dollhouse. So we had toys. We had a different kind. We had bicycles. We played all kind of games.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was-- this was later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not in your own family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, this was by Korczak.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- this was at Korczak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you remember-- and again, I know it's very-- you were so so little, so small. Do you remember any Jewish holidays in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all the holidays I remember. We used to celebrate Pesach, Seder. And we-- I would-- did ask the [NON-ENGLISH] with my brothers together. I remember Hanukkah. I remember all the Jewish holidays. It was very-- we-- nice to celebrate those holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate them just you and your-- your-- your parents and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were there-- did you have any extended family at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a big family. But we were very not often in touch with them. They lived far away or no communication. But I used to see mine grandmother very often, which used to live with her son, which was a rich man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your father's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she lived with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: With her son, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --father's brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about her? And anything about your uncle at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ugh. I remember very well. She was-- her face was wrinkled like a old potato. But she was a very smart, very good lady. She-- also, I think that she came to the [INAUDIBLE] of Hitler. She was alive, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: The uncle died before the war. And mine aunt died before the war. But the cousins that were in Poland, they probably died there. But one cousin is alive, the daughter of the rich man. She lives in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- do you remember from those very early years, did you have any special friends at all in the neighborhood, among the neighborhood children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to play on the street with children. But later, I didn't have a contact with them because I went in the-- the Korczak home. So I didn't have a contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know, as a-- as a young-- as a little child, did you know Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I understand, but I didn't speak. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Because everything-- everything in the house, the language in the house was Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your mother's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't move around. I remember that's the night when she died, I was next to her. When I woke up, my mother wasn't with me. And I started to cry, where is mommy? And my brother showed me that she is on the floor. She's dead. And she was covered with a linen. And I went down, and I cried and I uncovered and I said, the mother has no air. She will die. And my brother said, she is dead. She will not die anymore. She is dead. And this was all. But I remember her face. I was, at the time, probably six or seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the funeral? Was-- was there a funeral that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was a Jewish funeral. Oh God, the children were staying behind. And the funeral in Poland wasn't in a casket. It was in [NON-ENGLISH] you know what's [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like shrouds?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a line-- in a linen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Linen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took both sides. And we were behind. And the head fell out of it. And the eyes opened. This I will never forget. The linen was probably weak. But the head fell out. Then I start to cry, my mommy is alive. That's all I remember. This was the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERS) OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went-- you-- your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we did. And with my brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was it, uh, until your father remarried?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably not quite a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know the woman? Had he known the woman before?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. She was a little woman. She was never married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that uh, Matl that you'd mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Matl. Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Matl, M-A-T-L--

speaker: interviewee
text: L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: M-A-T--

speaker: interviewee
text: Matla. Matla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Matla. Matla. And she had never been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was her first marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not good. I used to take care of the baby when she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she and your father soon had a baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I used to take care of the baby. The baby was a very good baby. And uh, she wasn't good to me. And except this, I didn't have the right things which I need in the home. So a lady used to come to us, a friend of my mother. And she tried to get me to Korczak home. And she succeeded.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the-- the things that you didn't-- you know, in other words, like clothes, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was dressed poorly. But I got-- I was dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I went to Korczak, there was beautiful clothing for every child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- do you-- the baby's name, the baby-- that-- that-- your stepsisters name, do you recall the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Think. A long time ago. I will recall. Simaa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Simaa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Simaa. S-I-M-A-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She probably-- in time of war, I want to take her out with me. But mine stepmother said, OK, you can take her if she will be all the time with you. And I said, I cannot take her all the time with me. Because the first time now I'm in Poland, I came from Bialystok here to help. But I cannot take her with me because I have no steady place there. So she didn't allow her to take. She was a very smart, very intelligent, girl. She was the first girl in Poland to go to a high school-- Polish high school, the first Jewish girl. It was done by Pilsudski, Marshall Pilsudski. She wrote to him a letter and a poem. And he called the high school to take her. She was the first Jewish girl in the high school, which name was [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- she died in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Time of war, yeah. How she died, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your stepmother, the stepmother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Too. And my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father. Uh, ha-- before you-- well, well-- as far as the year that you went to the Korczak orphanage, when-- do you recall what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1925, I think. 1924 or '25.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about 10 years old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the friend of your mother persuaded your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the stepmother-- right. --to allow you to go. Where was the orphanage? In-- in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw. [? Krochmaina ?] 92. It was a beautiful big house. In the house were 56 girls, 50 boys, 20 people personnel which helped students, which teach-- went to schoo-- to school to learn how to be with children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to the orphanage, had you ever heard of Doctor Korczak?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And uh, when you came to the orphanage, do you remember the-- the first day? Do you remember going there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember. When I came there they took my hair off. And I was crying. And this was the rule. Every child which came to the orphanage, they had to be shaved off. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I cried because I have no hair. But it was nice. I didn't-- I didn't feel any uh, anger that my father gave me there. Because I was happy. The food was beautiful. The children were very good. I was very happy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when was the first time that you saw Doctor Korczak?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day when I came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he greet you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He called every child [POLISH]. [POLISH], It's like a little chicken. So I thought that I'm the only one he called [POLISH]. But I heard he called everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him one time, Doctor, why do you call everybody [POLISH]? He said, everybody to me is [POLISH]. Those are my children. What was your first impression of Dr. Korczak?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to describe because he was so warm, so good, to every child that you cling to him right away. It's like a magnet, a very nice person, a very good person. So everybody loved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to the orphanage, you were in a Polish school. You were in a school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was a public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, in that public school any, anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too young. No. In school I didn't feel anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that Polish-- in the Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- in-- in the Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly-- it was a Jewish neighborhood with mostly Jewish children. So I didn't see the difference. And I didn't feel anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everything in that school was taught in Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, as far as the teachers, were there-- were there nice teachers? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were very nice. Mostly Jewish. Three t-- teachers were Polish. But they were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you came to the orphanage, you continued to go to that same school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The school was changed to another school, closer to the neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also-- it was also a Jewish neighborhood, [? Krochmaina ?] was in ghetto. The same thing, I didn't feel any different about anti-Semitism. But when I was up at Mila, very often children were running after us. But otherwise, I didn't feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, in that when you were a-- a-- a-- a-- little child at home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in that immediate neighborhood. As far as the-- the orphanage, where did you sleep? Did you have a bedroom? Or was there a dormitory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. We had a big bedroom for 56 girls, a giant bedroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a dormitory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the middle there were two rooms. One side was for boys. The other side, for girls. In the middle was a room for somebody which watched the children at night. Every child had a responsibility to make their own bed, to clean the [BACKGROUND NOISE] place where they sleep. In the morning we had to shower. Then we go down. The bedrooms were on the third floor. So we have to go down to the second floor, where they gave all the children-- we used to drink [NON-ENGLISH], fish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cod liver oil?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cod liver oil. Dr. Korczak was standing there, watching. Every child should drink, and a little dry bread to eat after the drinking this. But very often the children didn't want to drink. But he was standing there and he made everybody to drink it. From there we went down to dining room. Every child has a number, the table where to sit. We sit at the table and we were served breakfast. And from there we used to go out to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After school we came back. We have to make homework. There was always somebody to help if you needed help. And after the homework, we went out to bicycling, or play uh, ball. And then lunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or uh, dinner? That-- it's supper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dinner, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Supper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Supper. Again, every child sit at the same table, at the same place. Every child knew where to sit. And every child has a number. The clothing was with the same number. The shoes, with the same number. Everything. So the children knew where to sit and what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: As far as the rules in the orphanage, did you have any say in what the rules would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every child has to do what the rules say. If a child wasn't behaving it was a court. It was just like a little state. We had the court. We had judges. We have children which rehabilitate, the children which didn't behave. And every child which had somebody watching him has to write a report. And the report was sent to Pani Stefa. She read it. If everything was OK, it's fine. If not, he was punished again. And he had a court order to behave. Otherwise he will go out of the house. We had one case. A boy was thrown out. We have paragraphs, which paragraph two says, if A did something wrong to B, the best thing is to forgive him. Because maybe he was aggravated by a family. Maybe somebody died or what. And to forgive him. Then is paragraph four. If the child did something very, very good, reward him. Paragraph one, he did a bad thing. We hope that he will be rehabilitated. Paragraph 900, it is very bad. He has to have somebody which rehabilitate him. If not, he will go out. I remember only one case, a boy was thrown out. Otherwise, it was beautiful, just like a country for-- for itself. Every child had the responsibility. And every child behaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Pani Stefa. Who was Pani Stefa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pani Stefa was a older lady. She was never married. I think that she met Dr. Korczak in Paris. And they came together. And they start-- he start to build the-- the Korczak home. And she was working with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wilczynska, Stefania Wilczynska. She came from a-- a nice home. Not very Jewish educated, she was with a Jewish heart. She was a very nice lady. I remember her father when he died, we were all going to the cemetery. She was tall. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see the-- uh, any interaction between Janusz Korczak and Pani Stefa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She just used to take care of him. When he went out he used to holler, [NON-ENGLISH], you didn't take the shawl! But put on the hat. It's raining. It's cold. Otherwise, no. But we know that they're-- were close a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who, as far as your friends in the orphanage, uh, who were your best friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My best friends are two. One is in Israel. Which I am in contact with her. There is a picture of her. And the other-- I had lots of friends. But two were very close. One is in Israel alive, and the other died in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the uh, Jewish atmosphere in the-- in the orphanage like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish atmosphere. Jewish holidays, we celebrated. We have a teacher of dancing. We have a teacher of gym. We have a teacher of Hebrew. And we were very active. The Jewish education was very high. Every morning Dr. Korczak used to go to the hall where we prayed in the morning. He prayed with us together. So we were educated in Jewish tradition. We have Seders. We have Hanukkahs. All the holidays were celebrated there.
